title: Tits's Dream: Buildings Over F1 and Combinatorial Flag Varieties
speaker: Oliver Lorscheid
abstract: The theme of the lecture is the notion of points over F1, the field with one element. Several heuristic computations led to certain expectations on the set of F1-points: for example the Euler characteristic of a smooth projective complex variety X should be equal to the number of F1-rational points of a suitable F1-model. While a literal interpretation of F1-points as morphisms from Spec F1 into X gives the wrong answers, we find a natural interpretation in terms of points over the Krasner hyperfield. This leads to a natural realization of Tits's proposed geometries over F1 as the "tip of the iceberg" inside Borovic-Gelfand-White's combinatorial flag varieties. As far as time allows, we explain how this phenomenon extends to an interpretation of structure constants of cluster algebras in terms of quiver matroids. Namely, the Caldero-Chapoton formula yields an explicit description of the cluster algebra of a tame quiver in terms of elementary expressions in the Euler characteristics of associated quiver Grassmannians. The aforementioned principle (which is proven for these instances) identifies the Euler characteristics with the number of certain quiver matroids, which are diagrams of matroids and strong maps.

proof_stages.label: Tits's q-to-1 shadow philosophy
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from Tits's observation that buildings over finite fields have Coxeter-complex shadows whose polynomial incidence invariants and Weyl-group symmetries look like the q=1 specialization.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial flag varieties as the matroidal replacement
proof_stages.summary: Flag matroids, following Borovik-Gelfand-White, are proposed as richer combinatorial flag varieties containing the Coxeter complex and receiving realization maps from ordinary buildings, but with ordinary symmetry groups too small.

proof_stages.label: Band schemes as the F1-geometric language
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls bands, their null-set replacement for addition, basic examples such as rings and the initial F1-plus-minus object, and the functor-of-points viewpoint for band schemes.

proof_stages.label: Grassmannians and flag varieties over bands
proof_stages.summary: Grassmannians and flag varieties are defined over bands by Plucker and incidence relations, recovering classical linear subspaces over fields and yielding matroids or flag matroids over the Krasner hyperfield.

proof_stages.label: Simplicial band scheme recovers buildings and combinatorial flag varieties
proof_stages.summary: All flag varieties of varying type are assembled via projection maps into a simplicial band scheme whose field-valued points give buildings, whose Krasner-valued points give combinatorial flag varieties, and whose closed points recover the Coxeter complex.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic crowds as generalized symmetry objects
proof_stages.summary: To replace insufficient ordinary automorphism groups, groups are generalized to crowds with ternary product-to-one relations, and algebraic crowds such as SL_n recover ordinary algebraic groups over rings while their Krasner closed points recover Weyl groups.

proof_stages.label: Crowd activities on flag varieties
proof_stages.summary: The natural SL_n action on classical Grassmannians and flag varieties is lifted to a crowd activity, giving the usual building action over fields, a new multivalued symmetry structure on combinatorial flag varieties over Krasner, and the standard S_n action on the Coxeter complex after passing to closed points.

examples.name: Omega S3 flag-matroid picture with the extra edge
examples.note: The small combinatorial flag variety of rank-one and rank-two matroids on three elements exhibits an extra non-realizable flag edge, which visibly destroys ordinary building symmetries and motivates the later generalized symmetry notion.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the Omega S3 flag-matroid picture, locates the extra uniform-matroid edge, explains why it is not representable over F2 as a flag, and notes its effect on symmetries

passes.reason: The extra uniform-matroid edge is used to show that the F2 building picture is not the whole flag-matroid picture and that the expected building symmetries fail.
